Core Meaning: The degree to which a measurement, estimate, or forecast is close to the true or accepted value.
Expansion: Accuracy describes closeness to correctness rather than consistency of repeated results. In project work it is used when discussing estimates, measurements, data quality, and reporting reliability. PMI distinguishes it from precision, which concerns the level of exactness or granularity rather than closeness to reality.
Contrast: Precision — accuracy is closeness to the true value, while precision is the degree of exactness or repeatability.
Example: A cost engineer estimates foundation work at 1.02 million and the final verified cost is 1.00 million, so the estimate is considered highly accurate even if it was not expressed to many decimal places.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition, Glossary, Accuracy, page 237

Core Meaning: The cost of evaluating products, services, or results to determine whether they conform to requirements.
Expansion: Appraisal cost is one part of the cost of quality. It covers inspection, testing, audits, and other evaluation activities used to detect defects or nonconformance. PMI distinguishes it from prevention cost and failure cost because it is spent to assess quality rather than to build quality in upfront or to correct defects after they occur.
Contrast: Prevention cost — appraisal cost is spent to evaluate conformance; prevention cost is spent to avoid defects before they occur.
Example: A software project pays for integration testing and independent quality audits, and those evaluation expenses are counted as appraisal cost.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Glossary, Appraisal Costs, page 695

Core Meaning: A quality measurement that records whether each inspected item simply has or lacks a given characteristic.
Expansion: Attribute sampling is a quality control method that evaluates each unit on a pass/fail or present/absent basis for a particular attribute, rather than measuring a continuous value. The result is conforming or nonconforming, which makes it useful for go/no-go decisions. It contrasts with variables sampling, which measures the degree of conformance on a scale.
Contrast: Variables sampling - variables sampling measures a continuous quantity (e.g., length in millimeters), whereas attribute sampling only notes whether a characteristic is present or absent.
Example: Inspecting a batch of bolts and recording each simply as 'defective' or 'not defective.'
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality control measurements

Core Meaning: A structured and independent examination used to determine whether project activities comply with policies, processes, and procedures.
Expansion: An audit reviews how work is being performed against defined rules, standards, or governance expectations. It focuses on process compliance, control effectiveness, and opportunities for improvement rather than only on the technical quality of a single output. In PMI practice, audits appear in quality assurance, procurement, governance, and compliance contexts.
Contrast: Inspection — an audit examines process compliance and control; an inspection examines the product or deliverable itself.
Example: An internal quality audit checks whether change requests, approvals, and procurement records were handled according to the project management plan and organizational policy.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, quality audit in Manage Quality tools and techniques, page 288

Core Meaning: The use of software tools to execute predefined tests against a product automatically and report the results.
Expansion: Automated testing runs repeatable checks such as unit, integration, and regression tests without manual effort, giving fast feedback on quality. It is central to continuous integration, where every change is verified before it is merged. Automation frees people for exploratory work and reduces the cost of frequent releases.
Contrast: Differs from manual testing, where a person executes each test step; automated testing executes scripted checks at machine speed.
Example: Each code commit triggers an automated test suite that blocks the merge if any regression test fails.
Source: Agile Practice Guide

Core Meaning: A comparison technique used to evaluate practices or performance against internal or external reference points in order to identify improvement opportunities.
Expansion: Within PMI business analysis guidance, benchmarking is used during current state assessment to compare an organisation's practices, outcomes, or capabilities with relevant peers or standards. It helps reveal performance gaps, useful practices, and realistic targets for improvement. In project environments, it is often applied when shaping process improvements, capability development, or solution expectations.
Contrast: Gap analysis — benchmarking compares current performance with peers or reference standards; gap analysis compares current state with a defined desired future state.
Example: A PMO benchmarks its schedule variance and delivery lead times against similar organisations to identify where governance and planning practices need improvement.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A structured form used to collect and tally data consistently so that patterns, frequency, or defect types can later be analysed.
Expansion: A check sheet is one of the basic quality tools used to capture observations at the point of work in a simple, repeatable format. It supports fact-based quality analysis by making data collection visible and consistent across time, shifts, locations, or categories. In project settings it is commonly used for defect counts, error types, inspection findings, or recurring process issues.
Contrast: Checklist — a check sheet collects and tallies observed data; a checklist verifies whether required steps or items have been completed.
Example: A manufacturing project team uses a check sheet during inspections to count each occurrence of paint defects by defect category across production batches.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The structured process of verifying and documenting that installed systems function as designed before handover to operations.
Expansion: Commissioning tests equipment and integrated systems against design intent and acceptance criteria, often progressing from component checks to full operational trials. It confirms safety, performance, and readiness for use, and produces records that support the operations team. Successful commissioning is frequently a precondition for final acceptance and benefit realization.
Contrast: Differs from construction completion, which means the asset is built; commissioning proves the built asset actually works as intended.
Example: After a new water treatment plant is built, the team runs commissioning trials and only then hands it to the operator.
Source: Construction and engineering practice

Core Meaning: A source of variation that is inherent in a stable process rather than arising from a specific unusual event.
Expansion: In project management and quality management, common cause refers to the normal, expected variation built into the way a process currently operates. It indicates that performance differences are coming from the system itself, not from an isolated defect or one-off disturbance. The practical implication is that improving results usually requires changing the process, not reacting to individual outcomes as if each were a separate problem.
Contrast: Special cause — common cause variation comes from the system as designed, while special cause variation comes from a specific, identifiable circumstance.
Example: A testing team sees small day-to-day fluctuations in defect detection rates because workload, test complexity, and reviewer capacity naturally vary within a stable range.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The degree to which a result meets established quality requirements, specifications, or standards.
Expansion: PMI defines conformance as the degree to which results meet the set quality requirements. The term is central to quality management because it focuses attention on whether deliverables actually satisfy agreed criteria rather than merely being completed. It is often discussed alongside nonconformance and the cost of quality when evaluating prevention, appraisal, and defect-related outcomes.
Contrast: Compliance — conformance is about meeting defined quality requirements; compliance is about meeting laws, regulations, or mandated rules.
Example: A medical-device project verifies that a prototype meets all specified tolerance levels and acceptance criteria before approving it for the next stage.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, p. 15

Core Meaning: Work performed to prevent defects and meet quality requirements correctly the first time.
Expansion: Conformance work is the effort spent up front to do project work correctly and avoid defects. It consists of prevention activities (such as training, documented processes, and good design) and appraisal activities (such as inspection and testing). Investing in conformance work reduces the more expensive nonconformance work of fixing failures later.
Contrast: Nonconformance work - nonconformance work deals with the consequences of failures after they occur, whereas conformance work prevents and detects them up front.
Example: Training the team, running peer reviews, and inspecting deliverables before handover are conformance work that reduces later rework.
Source: PMBOK Guide - cost of quality

Core Meaning: An ongoing effort to improve products, services, or processes through incremental learning and change.
Expansion: Continuous improvement is the practice of repeatedly making small, evidence-based enhancements instead of waiting for a major redesign. PMI connects it to retrospectives, lessons learned, quality management, and team learning across the life cycle. It matters because projects surface defects, delays, and coordination issues that can often be improved while work is still underway. The concept applies in predictive, agile, and hybrid environments.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Continuous improvement is a disciplined pattern of reviewing results, identifying causes, and adjusting how work is performed. It is ongoing and iterative, not a one-time workshop or end-of-project exercise.

Why it matters and where it applies: It helps teams improve quality, reduce waste, and increase delivery reliability while the project is still active. It applies in team practices, delivery workflows, governance routines, and handoffs to operations.

The most common misconception: that continuous improvement means changing processes constantly. In practice, it means making targeted changes that are tested, useful, and tied to better outcomes.

How it shows up in real projects: teams run retrospectives, analyze root causes, refine templates, adjust workflows, and track whether those changes reduce rework or cycle time.
Contrast: Lessons learned — continuous improvement is the ongoing practice of improving performance; lessons learned are the insights captured to inform that improvement.
Example: After repeated defects in requirements handoff, a project team adds a short peer review step and then tracks whether the change lowers rework over the next month.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A graphic display of process data over time and against established control limits, with a centerline that assists in detecting trends of plotted values toward either control limit.
Expansion: A control chart is used to assess whether a process is stable and behaving within expected variation. By plotting results over time, it helps the team distinguish normal variation from signals that suggest a special cause or loss of control. In projects, it is most often associated with quality control, though it can also support monitoring of other recurring performance data.
Contrast: Histogram — a control chart shows process behavior over time against control limits; a histogram shows the frequency distribution of values.
Example: A testing team tracks weekly defect escape rates on a control chart to see whether product quality is remaining within expected limits.

Core Meaning: The upper and lower boundaries of acceptable variation in a process or performance result before investigation or action is indicated.
Expansion: Control limits are thresholds used in quality and performance analysis to distinguish expected variation from signals that a process may be out of control. In PMI usage they are commonly associated with control charts and statistical process control. They matter because they help project teams avoid overreacting to normal fluctuation while still identifying non-random variation that warrants investigation.
Contrast: Specification limits — control limits reflect the natural performance boundaries of a process; specification limits reflect required tolerance or acceptance criteria for the product or result.
Example: A manufacturing project tracks component thickness on a control chart and investigates the production line when measurements trend toward the upper control limit even though final units still meet specification.
Source: PMBOK Guide quality management tools and control chart terminology; PMI Lexicon — exact page citation not retrievable from current source

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring and recording results of executing the quality activities to assess performance and recommend necessary changes.
Expansion: Control Quality checks deliverables and work results against quality requirements and standards. It uses inspection, testing, and related verification methods to identify defects, measure compliance, and support corrective action. PMI treats it as a monitoring and controlling process focused on outputs and performance results rather than on designing or improving the quality management processes themselves.
Contrast: Manage Quality — Control Quality evaluates deliverables and results; Manage Quality focuses on improving and assuring the processes used to create them.
Example: A software project team executes test cases on a release candidate, records defects, and verifies that acceptance criteria are met before deployment approval.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Control Quality; exact page not available in provided results

Core Meaning: A statistical relationship in which two variables tend to change together.
Expansion: Correlation measures the strength and direction of association between variables, helping analysts spot relationships in performance or risk data. A key caution is that correlation does not establish causation; a third factor may drive both.
Contrast: Causation — correlation shows two things move together; causation shows one actually produces the other.
Example: Analysis shows schedule slippage correlates with the number of open change requests.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: A statistical technique used to evaluate the degree to which two variables change together in a project context.
Expansion: In project management, correlation analysis helps examine whether changes in one variable are associated with changes in another, such as scope change frequency and schedule variance. It is used within quantitative analysis to support forecasting, risk analysis, and performance interpretation. A correlation indicates association, not proof that one factor causes the other.
Contrast: Regression analysis — correlation analysis measures the strength and direction of association, while regression analysis models how one variable predicts another.
Example: A project controls analyst tests whether rising defect counts are associated with increased rework effort across monthly reporting periods.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Money spent during the project to prevent defects and to meet quality requirements.
Expansion: Cost of conformance is the part of the cost of quality spent up front to avoid failures. It combines prevention costs (training, documenting processes, quality planning) and appraisal costs (testing, inspection, audits). Spending sensibly on conformance generally lowers the larger cost of nonconformance incurred when defects slip through.
Contrast: Cost of nonconformance - cost of conformance is spent up front to avoid failures, whereas cost of nonconformance is incurred after failures occur, through rework and warranty costs.
Example: Spending on quality training, written procedures, and pre-release inspections is all cost of conformance.
Source: PMBOK Guide - cost of quality

Core Meaning: The portion of cost of quality spent because work fails to meet requirements, covering internal and external failure costs.
Expansion: Cost of nonconformance is the money spent as a result of failing to meet quality requirements, and it forms one half of the cost of quality. It comprises internal failure costs such as rework and scrap found before delivery, and external failure costs such as warranty claims, liabilities, recalls, and lost reputation found after delivery. It contrasts with the cost of conformance - prevention and appraisal spending - and the central argument is that investing in conformance up front is cheaper than paying for nonconformance later.
Contrast: Cost of conformance - cost of nonconformance is money lost to failures and rework, whereas cost of conformance is money spent on prevention and appraisal to avoid those failures.
Example: A project incurs cost of nonconformance when a delivered feature fails in production and the team pays for emergency fixes and customer credits.
Source: PMBOK Guide - cost of quality (cost of nonconformance)

Core Meaning: All costs incurred over the life of the product by investment in preventing nonconformance to requirements, appraising the product or service for conformance, and failing to meet requirements.
Expansion: Cost of quality is the total cost view of achieving and not achieving quality. It includes the cost of prevention and appraisal as well as the cost of internal and external failure. PMI uses the concept to show that spending appropriately on quality upfront can reduce much larger downstream costs caused by defects, rework, and dissatisfied customers.
Deep Dive:
Cost of quality is a framework for understanding the full economic impact of quality-related decisions. It groups quality spending into categories:

- Prevention — money spent to stop defects from occurring.
- Appraisal — money spent to evaluate conformance to requirements.
- Internal failure — costs of defects found before the deliverable reaches the customer.
- External failure — costs of defects found after delivery.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often underestimate the hidden cost of poor quality, especially when rework, delays, warranty exposure, or operational disruption occur later. This concept matters in planning, quality management, procurement, and decisions about how much to invest in prevention versus correction.

The most common misconception is that quality always costs more. In practice, poor quality is often far more expensive, and thoughtful prevention and appraisal frequently lower total cost over the life of the product or deliverable.

How it shows up in real projects: teams invest in reviews, training, testing, standards, and process discipline to reduce defects before release. When they do not, the result is often rework, schedule slippage, post-delivery fixes, and stakeholder dissatisfaction that cost more than the original preventive effort.
Contrast: Quality control — cost of quality is the full cost framework around achieving and missing quality; quality control is the activity of checking outputs for conformance.
Example: A software programme funds automated regression testing and code review because fixing defects before release is far cheaper than patching them in production.

Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, section 8.1.2.3, page 282

Core Meaning: A quality philosophy holding that the performance standard should be zero defects, achieved by preventing problems rather than inspecting them out.
Expansion: Philip Crosby argued that quality means conformance to requirements, that the only acceptable performance standard is zero defects, and that quality is free because preventing defects costs less than the rework, scrap, and warranty claims they cause. He stressed building quality in through prevention and doing it right the first time rather than relying on detection. Defects are not inevitable but stem from inattention to requirements and process.
Contrast: Juran's Quality Trilogy - Crosby targets an absolute zero-defect standard through conformance and prevention, whereas Juran frames quality as fitness for use managed through planning, control, and improvement.
Example: A PM invests in design reviews and automated testing so defects are prevented early, arguing the prevention cost is lower than late rework.
Source: Crosby - Quality Is Free (zero defects)

Core Meaning: The degree to which project deliverables and outcomes meet or exceed customer needs and expectations.
Expansion: In project management, customer satisfaction is an outcome measure tied to whether the project's product, service, or result is perceived as valuable and fit for purpose. It depends not only on technical conformance to requirements but also on usability, timeliness, communication, and stakeholder experience. PMI treats it as closely connected to quality, benefits realisation, and stakeholder engagement.
Contrast: Quality — quality concerns conformance to requirements and fitness for use, while customer satisfaction reflects the customer's overall perception of whether the project delivered value.
Example: After a CRM implementation goes live on time, the organisation surveys sales users and finds satisfaction improved because the system reduces duplicate data entry and speeds up account updates.
Source: PMI Lexicon / not page-confirmed from available source text

Core Meaning: The degree to which project data is accurate, complete, consistent, timely, and fit for its intended use in decision-making.
Expansion: Data quality concerns whether the information used across a project can be trusted for planning, reporting, forecasting, and control. In PMI practice, poor-quality data weakens estimates, obscures risk signals, and leads to flawed stakeholder decisions. It is especially relevant where projects depend on performance metrics, schedule updates, cost reporting, issue logs, and benefits tracking.
Contrast: Data representation — data quality is about the reliability and fitness of data; data representation is about how data is structured, formatted, or displayed.
Example: A project manager finds that earned value reports are inconsistent because team members enter progress updates late and use different cost codes, so the team standardises data-entry rules and validation checks.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An imperfection or deficiency in a project component or deliverable such that it does not meet its requirements or specifications.
Expansion: A defect is a specific form of nonconformance in which the delivered result fails to satisfy stated or expected quality criteria. PMI uses the term in quality management to distinguish an actual flaw in the product, component, or output from broader issues in process performance. Defects are typically identified through inspection, testing, review, or use and may lead to rework or defect repair.
Contrast: Nonconformance — a defect is a particular flaw in a deliverable or component; nonconformance is the broader condition of not meeting a requirement.
Example: During user acceptance testing, the team finds that a reporting screen displays incorrect totals when filters are applied, so the issue is logged as a defect.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Defect

Core Meaning: The examination of defects to identify patterns, causes, and corrective actions.
Expansion: Defect analysis looks at defect data to understand what is failing, why it is failing, and how to reduce recurrence. In PMI practice, it supports quality control, root cause analysis, and process improvement. It is commonly used when teams need to move from isolated defect fixes to systemic prevention.
Contrast: Defect repair — defect analysis investigates the defect and its cause; defect repair corrects the defect itself.
Example: After repeated test failures, the team analyzes defect logs and finds that most issues come from unclear interface requirements.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Activities that keep errors from being introduced into deliverables or processes rather than finding them after they occur.
Expansion: In PMI-aligned practice, defect prevention focuses on building quality into the work through sound processes, standards, training, and early verification. It is part of a prevention-oriented quality mindset in which the team reduces the likelihood of rework, waste, and downstream failure. It is especially relevant in planning and managing quality where the cost of preventing errors is often lower than the cost of correcting them later.
Contrast: Inspection — defect prevention aims to stop defects from occurring; inspection detects defects after work has already been performed.
Example: A project team introduces peer reviews, coding standards, and automated test checks before integration so common errors are avoided instead of discovered during user acceptance testing.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The action taken to correct a defect in a component or deliverable.
Expansion: Defect repair is the work performed to fix a discovered nonconformance so the product can meet requirements or quality expectations. It may involve rework, replacement, correction, or another approved remedy depending on the nature of the defect. In PMI usage, it is a specific response to a quality problem rather than a general performance adjustment.
Contrast: Corrective action — defect repair fixes a specific product defect; corrective action more broadly addresses the cause of a variance or nonconformance.
Example: During system testing, the team finds an error in tax calculations and updates the software so invoices are computed correctly before release.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Defect repair

Core Meaning: A four-step iterative model for continuous improvement: Plan-Do-Check-Act.
Expansion: The Deming Cycle, also called the PDCA or Shewhart cycle, is a repeating loop of Plan, Do, Check, and Act used to drive continuous improvement. You plan a change, do it on a small scale, check the results against expectations, and act by adopting, adjusting, or abandoning it. Each pass feeds learning into the next, steadily refining a process or product. It underpins much of modern quality management and many lean and agile practices.
Deep Dive:
The Deming Cycle is a simple, disciplined loop - Plan, Do, Check, Act - for improving any process through repeated small experiments rather than one large change. Walter Shewhart originated it and W. Edwards Deming popularized it during the twentieth-century quality movement.

It matters because it makes improvement systematic and evidence-based, turning vague intentions into measurable trials. It shows up across quality management, Kaizen, lean, and retrospective-driven agile work, wherever teams want to learn their way to a better process.

A common misconception is that PDCA is a one-time, start-to-finish project sequence. It is meant to cycle continuously: the Act step loops straight back into the next Plan, so improvement never formally ends.

On real projects one pass through the loop looks like this:

- Plan: choose a process tweak to pilot.
- Do: run the tweak in one iteration.
- Check: review the resulting metrics in a retrospective.
- Act: standardize the change or drop it, then begin the next experiment.
Contrast: DMAIC - DMAIC is a structured Six Sigma method (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) for improving existing processes, whereas PDCA is a lighter, general-purpose continuous-improvement loop.
Example: A team struggling with defects plans a new code-review step, runs it for one sprint, checks the defect rate, then standardizes the step after confirming the improvement.
Source: Deming/Shewhart Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle (quality management theory)

Core Meaning: A product design approach that optimises a design for a specific characteristic such as manufacturability, reliability, cost, serviceability, or safety.
Expansion: Design for X is a family of design approaches that deliberately emphasises one or more critical quality or lifecycle characteristics during product development. PMI uses it in the quality context as a way to build desired attributes into the design rather than relying only on inspection later. It helps teams align technical choices with operational needs, customer expectations, and downstream constraints such as production or maintenance.
Contrast: Design of experiments — Design for X optimises the product design for desired characteristics; Design of experiments tests how factors affect outcomes.
Example: A medical-device project applies design for manufacturability so the product can be assembled consistently at scale without driving up defect rates.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Glossary, Design for X, p. 706

Core Meaning: A statistical method for identifying which factors may influence specific variables of a product or process under development or in production.
Expansion: Design of experiments is a structured analytical technique used to test how different variables affect a desired outcome. PMI places it within quality management as a way to understand cause-and-effect relationships and optimise product or process performance. It is especially valuable when multiple inputs may interact and teams need evidence to choose the best configuration.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Design of experiments is a planned method for varying selected factors systematically and measuring their effects on an output. Instead of changing one thing at a time, it can reveal which variables matter most and whether combinations of variables interact.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects that involve product development, process improvement, testing, or quality optimisation often need more than trial and error. This method helps teams improve quality, reduce waste, and make defensible technical decisions in engineering, manufacturing, healthcare, and other data-rich settings.

The most common misconception: that design of experiments is only for specialists in advanced laboratory settings. In practice, the core idea is broadly applicable wherever teams need structured evidence about what drives performance.

How it shows up in real projects: a team tests different material types, temperatures, and assembly settings to determine which combination produces the best reliability with acceptable cost and cycle time.
Contrast: Design for X — Design of experiments analyses how variables influence outcomes; Design for X focuses the design on achieving a target characteristic such as manufacturability or safety.
Example: A manufacturing project tests several machine-speed and temperature combinations to find the setup that minimises defects while maintaining throughput.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Glossary, design of experiments, p. 706

Core Meaning: The five-phase Six Sigma improvement cycle: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control.
Expansion: DMAIC is the structured Six Sigma method for improving an existing process. Define frames the problem and goals, Measure baselines current performance, Analyze finds root causes, Improve implements and verifies solutions, and Control sustains the gains. It is data-driven and aims to reduce variation and defects.
Contrast: Deming Cycle - both are improvement cycles, but DMAIC is a rigorous, metrics-heavy Six Sigma method for existing processes, while the Deming Cycle is a simpler general-purpose plan-do-check-act loop.
Example: A team uses DMAIC to cut invoice errors, measuring the baseline rate, analyzing causes, piloting a fix, and adding controls to hold the gain.

Source: Six Sigma - DMAIC methodology

Core Meaning: Quality failures discovered by the customer after delivery.
Expansion: External failure costs are part of the cost of nonconformance and arise when defects reach the customer - through returns, warranty claims, recalls, or damage to reputation. They are usually the most expensive failures because they occur after the product has left the organization.
Contrast: Internal failures - internal failure costs are found by the team before delivery, whereas external failures are found by the customer afterward.
Example: A shipped product that customers send back for warranty repair generates external failure costs.
Source: PMBOK Guide - cost of quality (external failure costs)

Core Meaning: The cost incurred because a product, service, or result does not conform to requirements or expectations.
Expansion: Failure cost is part of cost of quality and covers the consequences of defects, errors, and nonconforming work. PMI distinguishes internal failure costs, which occur before release or handoff, from external failure costs, which occur after the customer receives the output. It helps teams quantify the financial effect of poor quality and balance prevention, appraisal, and rework decisions.
Contrast: Appraisal cost — failure cost is spent after nonconformance occurs; appraisal cost is spent to assess conformance before failure is discovered.
Example: A software team incurs failure cost when defects found after release require emergency fixes, customer support, and service credits.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — cost of quality discussion; specific page not confirmed in available PMI source

Core Meaning: A decomposition technique that helps trace an undesirable effect back to possible root causes.
Expansion: A cause-and-effect diagram organises possible sources of a problem so a team can explore why an issue is occurring rather than only reacting to symptoms. It is commonly used in quality management and risk analysis to structure thinking about categories such as methods, materials, equipment, people, or environment. The technique improves problem diagnosis and supports more targeted corrective or preventive action.
Contrast: Control chart — a cause-and-effect diagram helps identify possible reasons for a problem, while a control chart helps determine whether process performance is stable over time.
Example: After repeated defects in a manufacturing handoff, the team maps possible causes under training, tooling, documentation, and supplier inputs to identify the most likely root cause.

Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 698

Core Meaning: A diagram that shows the sequence of steps, decisions, and flows in a process.
Expansion: PMI refers to process flows as a technique for understanding how work moves through a current or future process. A flowchart is the common visual expression of that logic, showing activities, decision points, and handoffs. In project work, it helps teams analyse process performance, clarify requirements, and identify bottlenecks or unnecessary complexity.
Contrast: Network diagram — a flowchart maps process steps and decision flow; a network diagram maps activity dependencies for project scheduling.
Example: A project team maps the change request approval process as a flowchart and discovers two redundant review steps that delay implementation.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, Section 4.2.2.8 Process Flows, p. 70

Core Meaning: Diagramming the sequence of steps, inputs, and outputs of a process to understand and improve how work flows.
Expansion: A flowchart, also called a process map, is a graphical representation of the steps in a process, showing decision points, inputs, outputs, and the flow of activities from start to finish. In quality management it helps teams see where defects may arise, spot non-value-adding steps, and reason about the cost of quality. The SIPOC (suppliers, inputs, process, outputs, customers) variant is a common form used to frame process boundaries.
Contrast: Cause-and-effect diagram - a fishbone diagram traces the root causes of a single problem, whereas a flowchart maps the overall sequence of an entire process.
Example: A team flowcharts its invoice-approval process and discovers a redundant sign-off step that is causing delays.
Source: PMBOK Guide - flowcharts (data representation)

Core Meaning: A category or rank assigned to items that have the same functional use but different technical characteristics.
Expansion: In PMI usage, grade distinguishes between levels or classes of a product or component that serve the same basic purpose but differ in features, durability, performance, or other technical attributes. It is a design or specification choice, not a direct measure of whether the item is acceptable. This matters because a low-grade item can still be high quality if it fully meets its stated requirements, while a high-grade item can still be poor quality if it fails to conform.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Grade is a classification concept used to describe the intended level of capability, refinement, or technical specification of a product, material, or deliverable. It helps teams describe what kind of thing is being provided, not whether it was produced correctly.

Why it matters and where it applies: Grade matters in scope definition, procurement, quality planning, and stakeholder expectation management. Projects routinely choose among alternative grades to balance cost, performance, and business need.

The most common misconception: that grade and quality are the same. They are not; grade reflects the chosen specification level, while quality reflects conformance to that chosen specification.

How it shows up in real projects: teams select commercial-grade versus industrial-grade components, standard versus premium finishes, or basic versus enterprise-class software tiers, then manage acceptance against the requirements of the chosen grade.
Contrast: Quality — grade is the category or specification level chosen for an item, while quality is the degree to which that item meets its requirements.
Example: A facilities project selects commercial-grade flooring for an office renovation because it meets the use case and budget, then verifies installation quality against the commercial-grade specification.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Grade, p. 10

Core Meaning: A histogram is a vertical bar chart showing a distribution of numerical data.
Expansion: A histogram displays how often values fall within defined intervals so patterns in variation become visible. In project management it is used to analyse data such as defects, cycle times, or workload distribution. It helps teams see concentration, spread, and possible outliers in performance data.
Contrast: Bar chart — a histogram shows the distribution of continuous numerical data by intervals, while a bar chart usually compares discrete categories.
Example: A project team plots issue-resolution times in a histogram to see whether most tickets are being closed within the expected service window.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, section on data representation; exact page citation requires source verification

Core Meaning: A predefined point in a process where work must stop until a required review, approval, or inspection is completed.
Expansion: Hold points are used to control quality, compliance, and readiness before work proceeds. They are common in regulated, safety-critical, and construction environments where the next step cannot begin until an authority signs off. They help prevent irreversible work from continuing before required checks are complete.
Contrast: Milestone — a hold point is a stop-and-check control point; a milestone is a significant event or marker in the schedule.
Example: Before concrete is poured, the inspector must approve the rebar installation at a hold point.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A formal review activity used to examine a work product and determine whether it conforms to specified requirements or standards.
Expansion: Inspection is a verification activity in which deliverables or components are checked for correctness, completeness, and compliance against defined criteria. PMI uses the term in quality management and product validation contexts to support defect detection and acceptance decisions. It may involve measurement, examination, testing, or review depending on the nature of the deliverable. Effective inspection helps prevent defective outputs from progressing further into the project or operation.
Contrast: Audit — inspection examines the work product itself for conformance, while an audit evaluates whether processes are being followed and are effective.
Example: In a manufacturing project, the quality team inspects assembled units against tolerance specifications before approving them for shipment to the pilot customer.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition, term Inspection, page 371

Core Meaning: An integration test verifies that combined components or subsystems work together correctly through their interfaces and interactions.
Expansion: In project work, especially software, systems, and technical delivery, integration testing checks whether separately developed elements function properly once connected. Its purpose is to reveal defects in interfaces, data exchange, sequencing, dependencies, and end-to-end behaviour that unit-level testing may not expose. It is commonly used before broader system, user, or acceptance testing.
Contrast: System test — integration testing focuses on interactions between combined components, while system testing evaluates the complete integrated system against broader requirements.
Example: After separate teams build payment, inventory, and order modules, the project runs integration tests to confirm that an online order updates stock, triggers payment processing, and sends confirmation data correctly.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Defects found by the project team before the deliverable reaches the customer.
Expansion: Internal failure costs are a category of the cost of quality, covering rework, scrap, and re-testing needed to fix defects discovered internally before delivery. They are part of the cost of nonconformance, but catching defects internally is far cheaper than letting them reach the customer as external failures. Tracking them helps justify investment in prevention and appraisal.
Contrast: External failures - external failure costs arise when the customer finds the defect, whereas internal failure costs are incurred when the team catches it first.
Example: Reworking a software module that failed the team's own integration test is an internal failure cost.
Source: PMBOK Guide - cost of quality (internal failure costs)

Core Meaning: A family of international standards defining requirements and principles for quality management systems.
Expansion: ISO 9000 is a set of standards published by the International Organization for Standardization that establishes the vocabulary and principles of quality management, with ISO 9001 specifying certifiable requirements for a quality management system. Organizations adopt it to demonstrate consistent processes, customer focus, and continual improvement. Projects operating under such a system align their quality processes with its requirements.
Contrast: ISO 9000 sets organization-wide quality-system standards, while the quality management plan applies quality to a specific project.
Example: A manufacturer pursues ISO 9001 certification to assure clients that its production processes meet a recognized standard.
Source: International Organization for Standardization (ISO 9000 family)

Core Meaning: A framework defining quality management as three interconnected processes - quality planning, quality control, and quality improvement.
Expansion:
Joseph Juran held that managing for quality rests on three processes:

- Quality planning — designing products and processes to meet customer needs.
- Quality control — monitoring operations and holding the gains.
- Quality improvement — raising performance to new levels by attacking chronic problems.

Juran also defined quality as fitness for use, emphasising the customer's real needs over mere conformance to specification. The trilogy mirrors how organisations manage finances through budgeting, cost control, and cost reduction.
Contrast: Crosby's Zero Defects - Juran defines quality as fitness for use and accepts an economic balance point, whereas Crosby defines quality as conformance to requirements with an absolute goal of zero defects.
Example: A PM plans quality metrics up front (planning), inspects deliverables against them (control), and runs retrospectives to cut recurring defects (improvement).
Source: Juran - Juran on Planning for Quality (quality trilogy)

Core Meaning: A continuous improvement approach focused on making small, ongoing changes to improve processes and outcomes.
Expansion: Kaizen emphasizes regular, incremental improvement rather than waiting for large redesign efforts. In project environments, it often appears through retrospectives, workflow adjustments, and local process experiments that improve quality or flow over time.
Contrast: Continuous improvement — Kaizen is a specific improvement philosophy and practice pattern, while continuous improvement is the broader concept of ongoing enhancement.
Example: A PMO reviews weekly handoff delays between design and testing, then introduces a small checklist and review cadence that steadily reduces rework over the next month.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An improvement methodology that combines Lean's focus on eliminating waste with Six Sigma's focus on reducing variation and defects.
Expansion:
Lean Six Sigma merges two disciplines:

- Lean — removes non-value-adding waste and improves flow.
- Six Sigma — uses data and statistics to reduce process variation and defects.

Practitioners commonly apply the DMAIC cycle of define, measure, analyze, improve, and control to drive structured improvement. The combined approach aims to deliver faster, cheaper, and higher-quality outcomes.
Contrast: Six Sigma - Six Sigma alone concentrates on reducing variation and defects, whereas Lean Six Sigma adds Lean's elimination of waste and improvement of speed.
Example: A team uses Lean Six Sigma to cut both the waiting time and the error rate in a claims-processing workflow.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The degree to which a product, service, or result can be effectively and efficiently modified, repaired, or updated over time.
Expansion: Maintainability is a quality characteristic that affects how easily an output can be corrected, adapted, improved, or supported after delivery. In project management, it matters because design and implementation choices made during the project influence long-term operational effort and cost. It is commonly considered in requirements, quality planning, engineering decisions, and lifecycle thinking.
Contrast: Reliability — maintainability concerns how easily something can be restored or changed; reliability concerns how consistently it performs without failure.
Example: A software project adopts modular architecture and clear documentation so future teams can update features without extensive rework.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of translating quality requirements into executable quality activities and evaluating whether project processes are adequate to achieve the intended quality outcomes.
Expansion: Manage Quality focuses on building quality into the work rather than only inspecting results after the fact. It uses audits, process analysis, and continuous-improvement techniques to confirm that planned quality approaches are practical and effective. The process connects quality planning with day-to-day execution so that deliverables are produced through capable, controlled processes.
Contrast: Control Quality — Manage Quality focuses on improving and assuring the processes used to create deliverables; Control Quality focuses on inspecting and verifying the actual outputs.
Example: On an infrastructure project, the team reviews concrete testing procedures, audits supplier inspection steps, and updates work instructions before large-scale pours begin.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition and Process Groups: A Practice Guide, Manage Quality

Core Meaning: A quality tool that shows the strength of relationships among factors arranged in rows and columns.
Expansion: A matrix diagram is one of the seven quality management and control tools, used to analyze the relationships between two or more groups of items placed in the rows and columns of a grid. Symbols or values in the cells indicate the presence and strength of each relationship, helping teams prioritize. Common forms include the L-shaped, T-shaped, and Y-shaped matrices, depending on how many groups are compared.
Contrast: Prioritization matrix - a prioritization matrix ranks options against weighted criteria to choose among them, whereas a matrix diagram more broadly maps the strength of relationships between sets of factors.
Example: A team uses an L-shaped matrix diagram to map customer requirements against design features and see which features matter most.
Source: PMBOK Guide - matrix diagrams (quality management and control tools)

Core Meaning: The arithmetic average of a set of values, used in estimating and quality analysis.
Expansion: The mean is the sum of values divided by their count, giving a central measure of a data set. In project work it appears in PERT expected-duration estimates and as the centerline of a control chart.
Contrast: Unlike the median, which is the middle value, the mean is sensitive to extreme values that can skew it.
Example: Averaging optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic estimates with PERT weighting yields a mean activity duration.
Source: Statistical method applied in estimating and quality; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An activity that consumes time or resources without increasing the value of the project deliverable or outcome.
Expansion: Non-value-added activity refers to work that does not contribute directly to customer value, required compliance, or necessary project progress. In project management, identifying these activities helps improve flow, reduce waste, and increase efficiency. The concept is commonly used in lean, process improvement, and delivery optimisation contexts.
Contrast: Value-added activity — a value-added activity contributes directly to the product, service, or required outcome, while a non-value-added activity does not.
Example: A team manually re-enters the same project status data into three different systems each week even though only one system is used for reporting decisions.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A condition in which a product, result, or process fails to meet a specified requirement.
Expansion: Nonconformance occurs when actual work or output does not comply with agreed requirements, standards, or specifications. It may be discovered during inspection, testing, or review and often triggers corrective action or disposition decisions. PMI treats nonconformance as a quality issue that must be managed rather than ignored.
Contrast: Defect — nonconformance is the broader failure to meet a requirement; a defect is a specific flaw in a product or component.
Example: During inspection, a batch of installed cables is found to be the wrong gauge and is recorded as a nonconformance.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — nonconformance

Core Meaning: The cost of quality spent dealing with failures and rework because work was not done correctly the first time.
Expansion: Nonconformance work is part of the cost of quality, covering everything done to handle defects and failures, including internal rework and scrap and external warranty, returns, and liability. It is the money spent because deliverables did not meet requirements, in contrast to the proactive cost of conformance. Effective quality management invests in conformance so that nonconformance work trends toward zero.
Contrast: Conformance work - conformance work prevents defects through planning and inspection, whereas nonconformance work pays for the consequences of defects after they occur.
Example: The labor and materials needed to rework a batch of parts that failed inspection.
Source: PMBOK Guide - cost of quality

Core Meaning: Qualitative or quantitative information, records, or statements of fact that are based on observation, measurement, or testing and can be verified.
Expansion: Objective evidence is verifiable information used to demonstrate that a requirement, process, or result exists and meets stated criteria. It matters because project and quality decisions should rely on facts that can be checked rather than opinion or assumption. In practice, it appears in audits, inspections, tests, acceptance activities, and compliance reviews.
Contrast: Opinion — objective evidence can be verified through observation, measurement, or testing; an opinion is a judgment that may not be verifiable.
Example: For a construction project, concrete strength test results and signed inspection records serve as objective evidence that a structural requirement was met.
Source: Project Quality Management guidelines in PMI standards; exact page citation requires source confirmation

Core Meaning: A bar chart used to show which causes or categories contribute most to a problem so effort can be focused where it has the greatest effect.
Expansion: PMI describes Pareto diagrams as a histogram that can communicate the results of root cause analysis. In project quality work, the chart helps teams separate the 'vital few' causes from the less significant many. This makes it useful for prioritising corrective action when multiple defects, issues, or complaint types are present.
Contrast: Histogram — a Pareto chart orders categories to emphasise the most significant contributors; a histogram shows frequency distribution without that prioritisation purpose.
Example: A testing team uses a Pareto chart of defect categories and finds that three defect types account for most rework, so it targets those first.
