Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, Section 4.2.2.7 Pareto Diagrams, p. 69

Core Meaning: The principle that roughly 80 percent of effects come from 20 percent of causes.
Expansion: Pareto's law, the 80/20 principle, observes that a small number of causes typically account for the majority of results, problems, or defects. In quality management it directs attention to the vital few sources responsible for most issues, often visualized with a Pareto chart. Focusing improvement effort on that critical 20 percent yields the greatest return.
Contrast: Pareto chart - Pareto's law is the underlying 80/20 principle, whereas a Pareto chart is the ranked bar chart used to apply it to real data.
Example: Finding that 80 percent of customer complaints stem from 20 percent of the defect types, and fixing those first.
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality tools (Pareto principle)

Core Meaning: Examination of a person's work by a qualified colleague to find errors and improve quality.
Expansion: A peer review is a quality technique in which a coworker of similar expertise inspects completed work, such as requirements, code, or documents, to catch defects and confirm completeness. By bringing a second set of informed eyes before formal acceptance, it catches problems early and cheaply.
Contrast: Inspection - a peer review is an informal check by an equal, whereas a formal inspection follows a defined process and is often led by trained reviewers or auditors.
Example: A business analyst asking a colleague to review a requirements document before sending it to stakeholders.
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality control techniques

Core Meaning: The process of identifying quality requirements and standards for the project and documenting how to meet them.
Expansion: Plan Quality Management determines the quality requirements and standards that apply to the project and its deliverables and defines how compliance will be demonstrated. Its main output is the quality management plan, which guides later assurance and control work. Planning quality early helps embed prevention over inspection and avoids costly rework.
Contrast: Control Quality — Plan Quality Management defines the standards and approach, while Control Quality measures deliverables against those standards.
Example: For a medical device project, the team documents applicable regulatory standards and test criteria in the quality management plan.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Plan Quality Management

Core Meaning: The complete set of items or events from which a sample may be drawn for inspection.
Expansion: In statistical quality control the population is the entire group of units under consideration, such as all parts in a production run. Because inspecting every item is often impractical, a representative sample is taken to infer the quality of the population.
Contrast: A population is the whole, whereas a sample is a subset selected to represent it.
Example: All ten thousand printed circuit boards in a batch form the population from which fifty are sampled for testing.
Source: Statistical method applied in quality control; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Precision is the degree to which repeated measurements or estimates under unchanged conditions show the same or very similar results.
Expansion: In project management, precision is used when discussing data quality, estimating, measurement, and control. A precise result is consistent across repetitions, even if it is not perfectly correct. PMI distinguishes precision from accuracy because project decisions can be affected by data that is tightly clustered but still off target.
Contrast: Accuracy — precision is about consistency of repeated results; accuracy is about closeness to the true or correct value.
Example: A cost analyst produces three independent estimates for the same work package and all are nearly identical, showing high precision even before validating whether the estimate is accurate.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, p. 704

Core Meaning: Prevention refers to actions taken to keep errors, defects, or other unwanted outcomes from occurring in project work.
Expansion: Within PMI quality management, prevention focuses on designing and managing work so problems do not happen in the first place. It is generally more effective than relying only on inspection after the fact. Prevention supports process quality, lowers rework, and improves confidence in deliverables.
Contrast: Inspection — prevention aims to stop defects before they occur; inspection finds defects after work has already been performed.
Example: Before development begins, a team introduces coding standards, peer review checklists, and automated tests to prevent defects rather than depending only on final testing to catch them.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, p. 706

Core Meaning: The cost of activities performed to avoid defects and keep project outputs and processes within required quality standards.
Expansion: Prevention cost is part of the cost of quality and covers spending intended to stop errors before they occur. It includes actions such as training, process improvement, quality planning, and preventive reviews. PMI uses the concept to show that investing early in quality can reduce rework, failure costs, and downstream disruption.
Contrast: Appraisal cost — prevention cost is spent to avoid defects from happening; appraisal cost is spent to assess whether outputs already meet requirements.
Example: A project funds peer design reviews and staff training on coding standards to reduce defects before system testing begins.
Source: PMBOK Guide / Quality management references — prevention cost; exact standard page citation not available in retrieved PMI sources

Core Meaning: A quality principle that it is better to design and build quality in than to find and fix defects after the fact.
Expansion: Prevention over inspection holds that investing in good process, training, and design avoids defects, which is cheaper than detecting and reworking them later. It shifts cost from appraisal and failure toward prevention within the cost of quality. The principle underlies practices such as standardized procedures, mistake-proofing, and early testing.
Contrast: Contrasts with an inspection-led approach that relies on catching defects at the end; prevention stops defects from occurring.
Example: Rather than inspecting every finished part, the team improves the machining setup so defects rarely arise in the first place.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A technique for examining a process to identify inefficiencies, variation, and opportunities for improvement.
Expansion: Process analysis is used to study how a project-related process works and where it can be improved. PMI applies it to examine process steps, handoffs, bottlenecks, waste, and non-value-adding work so the team can improve quality and efficiency. It is commonly used in quality management and continuous improvement efforts.
Contrast: Root cause analysis — process analysis examines the design and performance of a process; root cause analysis focuses on why a specific problem occurred.
Example: A project team reviews its change control workflow, finds duplicate approvals that add delay, and redesigns the process to shorten turnaround time.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Process analysis

Core Meaning: The measurable ability of a process to produce outputs that meet specified requirements in a stable and predictable way.
Expansion: Process capability is used to judge whether a process can consistently achieve required performance levels, quality limits, or control thresholds. In project work it is most relevant to quality management, process improvement, and operational handoff, where leaders need evidence that a process will perform reliably. PMI commonly treats it as part of quality and performance thinking, even when the exact phrasing comes from broader quality-management practice.
Contrast: Process performance — process capability describes what a process is able to achieve under stable conditions; process performance describes how the process is actually performing now.
Example: A manufacturing-improvement project measures whether a packaging line can consistently fill units within tolerance before declaring the new process ready for rollout.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A representation of the sequence of activities, decisions, inputs, and outputs in a process.
Expansion: Process flow shows how work moves from one step to another, often including handoffs, decision points, and dependencies. In project management it supports planning, analysis, improvement, and communication by making the logic of the work visible. PMI materials use process-flow thinking in process descriptions, workflow analysis, and quality tools, even where the exact term may appear more as a technique than as a core glossary entry.
Contrast: Workflow — workflow usually emphasises the operational movement of tasks through people or systems; process flow is the mapped sequence and logic of the process itself.
Example: A PMO maps the change-control process from request submission through impact review, approval decision, implementation, and closure to identify bottlenecks.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A deliberate effort to enhance the efficiency, effectiveness, or quality of a process.
Expansion: Process improvement focuses on making a process work better by reducing waste, variation, delays, or defects. In PMI practice, it is commonly associated with quality management and process analysis, where teams examine how work is performed and identify opportunities to improve it. It can be applied to project processes, product-related processes, or organizational processes that affect project delivery.
Contrast: Process analysis — process improvement is the act of making the process better; process analysis is the examination used to find improvement opportunities.
Example: A project team streamlines its change approval workflow by removing duplicate reviews and shortening turnaround time.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — process improvement, page 8

Core Meaning: A visual representation of the sequence of steps, inputs, and outputs that make up a process.
Expansion: A process map depicts how work flows through a series of activities, showing the order of steps, decision points, handoffs, and the inputs and outputs at each stage. It is used to understand, communicate, and improve processes by exposing bottlenecks, redundancies, and sources of variation. Process mapping is a foundational technique in quality and continuous-improvement work.
Contrast: A SIPOC gives a high-level summary of suppliers and customers, while a process map details the internal steps between them.
Example: A team maps its change-request workflow and discovers two approval steps that add delay without adding value.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: How well a process delivers its intended outputs against measures such as quality, time, and cost.
Expansion: Process performance describes the actual results a process produces, judged against defined targets, control limits, or capability requirements. It is assessed using metrics and quality tools such as control charts to distinguish normal variation from problems. Understanding performance drives improvement and keeps the process stable and predictable.
Contrast: Process capability — process performance is what a process currently achieves, while process capability is its inherent ability to meet specifications when stable.
Example: A control chart shows the order-fulfilment process averaging two days with rising variation, prompting a review of process performance.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Manage Quality and process analysis

Core Meaning: The condition in which a process performs predictably over time and shows only expected, inherent variation rather than unusual disturbance.
Expansion: Process stability means a process is operating consistently enough that its performance can be understood, predicted, and improved rationally. In project quality management, it matters because unstable processes produce unreliable outputs and make root-cause analysis difficult. PMI-related quality practice treats stability as a prerequisite for meaningful control and continuous improvement.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Process stability describes a process whose variation stays within a consistent pattern caused by common, built-in factors rather than special disruptions. A stable process is not necessarily perfect, but it is predictable enough to analyse and manage.

Why it matters and where it applies: quality management depends on knowing whether performance changes are normal or signal a real problem. Stability matters in repetitive project work, testing, manufacturing-related projects, service transitions, and any environment where process control supports reliable delivery.

The most common misconception: that stable means good. A process can be stable and still perform poorly if it consistently delivers results outside stakeholder needs; stability only means the behavior is predictable.

How it shows up in real projects: a deployment process repeatedly completes within a narrow range of time and defect levels, allowing the team to forecast release readiness and improve the workflow with confidence.
Contrast: Process capability — process stability is about predictability over time; process capability is about whether the process can meet specification limits or performance requirements.
Example: A testing process produces a consistent defect-detection rate sprint after sprint, which lets the team identify whether a sudden drop is an actual issue or normal fluctuation.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The natural or assignable differences in process performance that cause outputs or results to vary over time.
Expansion: Process variation is the change observed in how a process performs or what it produces from one occurrence to another. In project quality management, understanding variation is essential for distinguishing routine fluctuation from signals that require intervention. PMI-related quality practice uses variation analysis to support control, forecasting, and continuous improvement.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Process variation refers to the fact that no process produces identical results every time. Some variation comes from common causes built into the system, while some comes from special causes such as breakdowns, mistakes, or external disruption.

Why it matters and where it applies: managers make better decisions when they know whether a result reflects random fluctuation or a meaningful shift. This applies in quality control, schedule performance of repetitive tasks, defect patterns, service response times, and production-oriented project environments.

The most common misconception: that every deviation from a target needs correction. Acting on normal variation can make performance worse, while failing to spot special-cause variation can leave real problems unresolved.

How it shows up in real projects: a team tracks cycle time on repeated work items and sees that most fluctuations are normal, but one major spike reveals an environment outage that needs corrective action.
Contrast: Process stability — process variation is the pattern of fluctuation in performance; process stability is the condition in which that variation is predictable and under control.
Example: A project team monitors turnaround time for change requests and uses the spread of results to distinguish normal workload effects from a breakdown in approvals.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The degree to which a product meets its intended functional, quality, and user expectations in operation.
Expansion: Product performance concerns how well the delivered product actually works against requirements, acceptance criteria, and stakeholder needs. It matters because project success is not only about delivering outputs on time and within budget but also about whether the resulting product performs effectively in use. PMI addresses this through product, quality, measurement, and value-delivery concepts rather than always as a single formal glossary term.
Contrast: Project performance — project performance concerns how the project is being managed and delivered; product performance concerns how the resulting deliverable performs for users or operations.
Example: A software project meets schedule targets, but the delivered platform still fails response-time thresholds under normal user load, revealing weak product performance.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The knowledge area that applies the organization's quality policy to plan, manage, and control project and product quality requirements.
Expansion: Project Quality Management comprises Plan Quality Management, Manage Quality (quality assurance), and Control Quality. It works to ensure the project satisfies the needs for which it was undertaken, emphasizing prevention over inspection and continuous improvement. It addresses both the quality of the project's deliverables and the quality of the management processes used.
Contrast: Manage Quality vs Control Quality - Manage Quality is process- and assurance-focused, while Control Quality inspects deliverables for conformance.
Example: A team writes a quality management plan, audits processes, and inspects deliverables against checklists to keep defects within tolerance.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Project Quality Management knowledge area

Core Meaning: The degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfills requirements.
Expansion: Quality in PMI terms is not simply excellence or luxury; it is conformance to requirements and fitness for intended use. It applies to both project deliverables and the processes used to create them, linking customer expectations, prevention, assurance, and control. Effective quality management reduces defects, rework, and dissatisfaction while increasing the likelihood that outcomes will be accepted and perform as intended.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Quality is the extent to which deliverables and processes meet defined requirements and are suitable for their intended purpose. It is managed through planning quality standards, building quality into the work, and checking results against those standards.

Why it matters and where it applies: Quality affects customer satisfaction, operational performance, compliance, safety, and total cost of delivery. It applies across all industries, from software and product design to construction, healthcare, and service transformation.

The most common misconception: that quality means adding premium features or inspecting defects out at the end. In practice, PMI treats quality as requirements-based and emphasises prevention, process capability, and continuous improvement over late detection alone.

How it shows up in real projects — teams:
- Define acceptance criteria
- Set quality metrics
- Conduct reviews and testing
- Analyse defects and root causes
- Adjust processes to improve consistency and reduce rework
Contrast: Grade — quality is how well requirements are met; grade is a category or rank assigned to deliverables with different technical characteristics.
Example: In a water-treatment plant project, quality means the installed system consistently meets regulatory purity thresholds and documented performance tolerances, not that it contains the most advanced optional equipment.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Quality; PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 8.1, p. 277

Core Meaning: A planned and systematic set of activities used to provide confidence that the project will satisfy relevant quality requirements.
Expansion: Quality assurance focuses on whether the processes used to create deliverables are appropriate, followed, and effective. It is preventive rather than detective, aiming to reduce defects by improving how work is performed. PMI distinguishes it from inspection-oriented activities by emphasizing process evaluation, audits, and continuous improvement.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Quality assurance is the process-focused side of quality management. It examines whether the methods, standards, and procedures used by the team are capable of producing outputs that meet requirements.

Why it matters and where it applies: strong quality assurance reduces rework, increases predictability, and improves stakeholder confidence. It is especially important in regulated, high-risk, or repeatable delivery environments such as construction, engineering, healthcare, and software development.

The most common misconception: that quality assurance means testing the final deliverable. Testing and inspection are usually part of quality control, while quality assurance asks whether the work system itself is sound and being followed.

How it shows up in real projects:
- Process audits
- Peer reviews of methods
- Compliance checks
- Lessons-learned driven process improvements
- Verification that teams are using approved standards and procedures
Contrast: Quality control — quality assurance focuses on improving and confirming the process used to create deliverables; quality control focuses on inspecting the deliverables themselves.
Example: On a medical device project, an internal audit checks whether design reviews, traceability, and verification steps are being performed according to the approved development process before release testing is completed.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 8.2 Manage Quality, p. 288

Core Meaning: A structured, independent review used to determine whether project activities comply with organizational and project policies, processes, and procedures.
Expansion: A quality audit examines how work is being performed, not just whether the final output meets requirements. It identifies good practices, gaps, inefficiencies, and opportunities for improvement in the quality management system. In PMI usage, it sits within quality assurance because it focuses on process compliance and process improvement rather than product inspection.
Contrast: Inspection — a quality audit reviews the process and compliance system; inspection checks the deliverable itself.
Example: On a pharmaceutical project, an internal auditor reviews whether design reviews, document control, and validation steps were performed according to the approved quality procedures.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, section 8.2.2.5, page 291

Core Meaning: Structured lists of items to inspect or steps to perform, used to verify that deliverables meet quality requirements.
Expansion: Quality checklists are component- or activity-specific lists created during Plan Quality Management and used in Control Quality to confirm that required steps were performed and that deliverables conform to standards. They promote consistency and completeness in inspections.
Contrast: Quality metrics - a checklist confirms steps and items, while a quality metric defines a measurable attribute and its tolerance.
Example: An installation team uses a checklist to confirm every wiring step before sign-off.
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality checklists

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring and recording results of executing the quality activities to assess performance and recommend necessary changes.
Expansion: Quality control focuses on the deliverables and work results to determine whether they meet quality requirements. It is primarily detective, using inspection, testing, and measurement to identify defects or nonconformance. PMI treats it as part of monitoring and controlling because it provides evidence for corrective action and process improvement.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Quality control is the product-focused side of quality management. It checks outputs against requirements and uses tools such as inspections, tests, and measurements to determine conformance.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps teams catch defects before delivery, reduce rework, and protect customer satisfaction. It is used in nearly every project type, especially where deliverable quality can be verified through testing or inspection.

The most common misconception: that quality control is the same as quality assurance. Quality assurance is process-focused and preventive, while quality control is output-focused and detective.

How it shows up in real projects:
- Test execution
- Inspection of completed work
- Defect logging
- Acceptance sampling
- Review of quality control measurements that triggers corrective action when results fall outside expectations
Contrast: Quality assurance — quality control checks deliverables and results; quality assurance evaluates and improves the process used to create them.
Example: A construction team inspects welded joints and performs load tests to confirm the finished structure meets specification before handover.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 8.3 Control Quality, p. 298

Core Meaning: The documented results of quality control activities used to assess performance and support decisions.
Expansion: Quality control measurements are the recorded outputs of inspections, tests, and other control activities. They provide evidence about whether deliverables or processes meet quality requirements and help identify trends, defects, or nonconformance. PMI uses them as inputs to quality reports and corrective action decisions.
Contrast: Quality metrics — quality metrics define what will be measured; quality control measurements are the actual recorded results of those measurements.
Example: A software team records defect counts, test pass rates, and response times as quality control measurements during system testing.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — quality control measurements, page 300

Core Meaning: The processes and activities used to translate quality policy into planning, assurance, control, and continuous improvement so project results meet relevant requirements and are fit for use.
Expansion: Quality management in PMI is broader than testing or defect detection. It includes defining the quality standards that matter, building them into project work, checking results against those standards, and improving the system that produces those results. Its purpose is not only conformance, but also ensuring that deliverables satisfy stakeholder needs and support intended benefits.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Quality management is the integrated approach for planning, managing, and controlling quality across the project life cycle. It connects standards, acceptance criteria, process discipline, verification activities, and improvement actions so that quality is designed into the work rather than inspected in at the end.

Why it matters and where it applies: poor quality drives rework, delays, cost growth, stakeholder dissatisfaction, and operational failure after handoff. It applies in predictive, agile, and hybrid environments alike, whether the deliverable is software, construction work, a service rollout, or an organisational change initiative.

The most common misconception: that quality management is mainly about final inspection or a specialist quality team. In practice, it starts during planning, belongs to the whole team, and is strongest when requirements, processes, and acceptance criteria are clear from the beginning.

How it shows up in real projects — teams:
- Define acceptance criteria
- Set quality metrics
- Perform reviews and testing
- Analyse defects and process variation
- Use lessons learned to reduce recurring problems across iterations or phases
Contrast: Quality control — quality management is the full system of planning, assurance, control, and improvement, while quality control focuses on monitoring and verifying results against requirements.
Example: In a hospital-system implementation, the project team defines data-accuracy thresholds, review procedures, test scripts, and defect-resolution workflows to ensure the new patient-record process is reliable before go-live.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — Quality management, pp. 22-23

Core Meaning: A set of techniques used to plan, analyze, and improve quality and to display data about process performance and defects.
Expansion: This group of tools supports quality planning, assurance, and control by helping teams investigate causes, visualize relationships, and prioritize problems. It traditionally includes cause-and-effect diagrams, flowcharts, check sheets, Pareto charts, histograms, control charts, and scatter diagrams, alongside management and planning tools such as affinity diagrams and matrix diagrams. Selecting the right tool depends on whether the goal is to gather, organize, or analyze quality data.
Contrast: The tools analyze data and causes, while a quality audit independently checks compliance with processes.
Example: A team uses a Pareto chart and a cause-and-effect diagram together to find the dominant source of defects.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Sixth Edition

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that describes how applicable policies, procedures, and guidelines will be implemented to achieve the quality objectives.
Expansion: The quality management plan explains how the project will plan, manage, and verify quality across its work and deliverables. It sets expectations for standards, metrics, roles, review activities, and acceptance approaches. In practice, it provides the operating rules for how the team will meet quality requirements throughout the project lifecycle.
Contrast: Quality assurance — the quality management plan defines how quality will be managed; quality assurance is one category of activity performed under that plan.
Example: A bridge project documents inspection hold points, material testing criteria, audit responsibilities, and defect escalation rules in its quality management plan before construction begins.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 8.1.3.1 Quality Management Plan, p. 282

Core Meaning: The organizational framework of policies, processes, and procedures used to plan, execute, and control quality.
Expansion: A quality management system (QMS) provides the structure through which an organization directs and coordinates its quality activities, often aligned to standards such as ISO 9000. On projects it supplies the policies, procedures, and resources that the quality management plan draws upon. A QMS supports continuous improvement and consistent, repeatable quality outcomes.
Contrast: Quality management plan - the QMS is the organization-wide framework, while the quality management plan applies and tailors it to a specific project.
Example: A manufacturer's ISO 9001 QMS supplies the documented procedures the project team follows for inspections and audits.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Manage Quality (organizational quality system)

Core Meaning: Project or product attributes and how they will be measured to verify compliance with quality requirements.
Expansion: Quality metrics define what aspect of quality will be measured and how the measurement will be taken. They make quality expectations observable and support consistent verification during quality assurance and quality control activities. Examples include defect rates, test coverage, customer satisfaction scores, and other measurable attributes tied to requirements.
Contrast: Quality requirements — quality metrics define how quality will be measured; quality requirements define what quality characteristics must be achieved.
Example: A software project tracks percentage of requirements covered by tests and number of defects found per day as quality metrics.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 8.1.3.2 Quality Metrics, p. 282

Core Meaning: Specific, measurable outcomes that define the required level of quality for project deliverables and processes.
Expansion: Quality objectives translate stakeholder needs, requirements, and acceptance expectations into concrete targets that the project can plan, manage, and verify. They help the team decide what quality means in practical terms, such as defect thresholds, performance levels, compliance criteria, or service levels. In PMI practice, they are used to guide quality planning, quality assurance, and quality control so that fitness for use and conformance to requirements can be assessed consistently.
Contrast: Quality requirements — quality objectives state the target outcomes to be achieved, while quality requirements specify the detailed criteria or conditions that deliverables must meet.
Example: In a hospital-system implementation project, the team sets quality objectives that patient-record retrieval must complete within two seconds, critical defects must be zero at go-live, and user training satisfaction must exceed 90%.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide — not directly listed as a formal PMI glossary term; aligns with project quality planning concepts

Core Meaning: The overall intentions and direction of an organization with regard to quality, as formally expressed by top management.
Expansion: A quality policy states the organization-wide commitment to quality and provides the framework for quality objectives. It guides how projects define and manage quality expectations, standards, and responsibilities. In PMI usage, it is an organizational input that shapes project quality planning and execution.
Contrast: Quality management plan — the quality policy states the organization-wide direction on quality; the quality management plan explains how a specific project will implement it.
Example: A construction firm has a quality policy requiring approved materials and documented inspections, and the project team builds those controls into execution plans.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Quality policy

Core Meaning: A project document that records quality management issues, quality control measurements, and recommendations for corrective action.
Expansion: A quality report summarises the results of quality activities and highlights trends, exceptions, and actions needed. It helps stakeholders understand whether quality objectives are being met and where corrective or preventive action may be required. In PMI usage, it is a communication and control artifact within project quality management.
Contrast: Quality control measurements — quality control measurements are the raw data; a quality report interprets and communicates that data.
Example: A software project issues a weekly quality report showing defect density, test pass rates, and open critical defects along with recommended fixes.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, section 8.3.3.2, page 300

Core Meaning: A condition or capability used to assess conformance by validating the acceptability of an attribute for the quality of a result.
Expansion: A quality requirement specifies the standard a deliverable's attribute must meet to be acceptable, giving an objective basis for validation. It is captured during requirements collection and quality planning and verified during Control Quality.
Contrast: Acceptance criteria - quality requirements define attribute conformance, while acceptance criteria are the broader set of conditions for a deliverable to be accepted.
Example: A requirement that a web page load in under two seconds is a quality requirement validated by performance testing.
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality requirement

Core Meaning: The documented conditions or criteria that a product, service, or result must satisfy to meet stakeholder expectations and project objectives.
Expansion: Quality requirements specify the standards or characteristics that the deliverable must meet. They are used to guide planning, testing, inspection, and acceptance decisions. In PMI practice, they are distinct from quality metrics, which describe how those requirements will be measured.
Contrast: Quality metrics — quality requirements state what must be achieved; quality metrics state how it will be measured.
Example: A medical device project defines sterility, reliability, and traceability requirements that the final product must satisfy before release.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 8.1.3.3 Quality Requirements, p. 282

Core Meaning: An analytical method in which input variables are examined in relation to their corresponding output variable.
Expansion: Regression analysis is used to understand the relationship between one dependent variable and one or more independent variables. In project management, it can support estimating, forecasting, and trend analysis by showing how changes in inputs relate to outcomes. PMI includes it as a quantitative analytical method for examining patterns in project data.
Contrast: Correlation analysis — regression analysis examines how input variables relate to an output variable; correlation analysis measures the strength of association between variables without modeling prediction.
Example: A project team analyzes whether defect counts are related to code size, test coverage, and developer experience to improve future estimates.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, regression analysis

Core Meaning: The degree to which a system, product, service, or component performs its intended function consistently under stated conditions for a specified period of time.
Expansion: Reliability concerns dependable performance over time, not just initial correctness at the moment of delivery. In project management, it often appears as a quality, engineering, product, or operational requirement that influences design decisions, testing strategy, risk response, and acceptance criteria. It is especially important in projects involving infrastructure, software, manufacturing, healthcare, transportation, defense, and any environment where failure has material consequences.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Reliability is the likelihood that an item will continue to perform as intended over a defined period and under defined conditions. It focuses on stability and consistency in use, not merely whether the item works once.

Why it matters and where it applies: reliability drives stakeholder confidence, lifecycle cost, safety, and service continuity. It is central in projects that produce assets or systems expected to operate predictably after handoff, especially where downtime, failure, or maintenance burden create major business impact.

The most common misconception: that reliability is the same as quality in general or the same as availability. Reliability is a specific performance attribute over time; a product may be high quality in many respects yet still fail too often, and a system may be available through redundancy without each component being highly reliable.

How it shows up in real projects: teams translate reliability needs into requirements, test regimes, design tolerances, maintenance assumptions, risk analyses, and vendor criteria. It appears in measures such as failure rates, mean time between failures, warranty expectations, and service-level commitments.
Contrast: Availability — reliability concerns consistent failure-free performance over time, while availability concerns whether the system is up and usable when needed.
Example: In a rail-signaling modernization project, the sponsor requires the installed control system to maintain consistent operation with extremely low failure rates, so the team designs redundant components, runs endurance testing, and sets acceptance thresholds tied to operational performance.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition — reliability is treated as a product and quality characteristic; confirm exact glossary citation in PMI Lexicon

Core Meaning: An assessment of a product, document, or project work to determine whether it is complete, correct, and acceptable.
Expansion: A review is a structured examination of a work product, deliverable, or project status to judge quality, completeness, or readiness. In PMI practice, reviews are used to identify defects, confirm alignment with expectations, and support decisions before work proceeds. They may be formal or informal depending on governance needs and delivery approach. Reviews appear across quality management, requirements work, governance, and iterative delivery.
Contrast: Inspection — a review is a broader evaluation of work or status; an inspection is a more detailed check for conformance to specified criteria.
Example: Before releasing a training package, the team reviews the materials with subject matter experts and the sponsor to confirm accuracy and completeness.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — review

Core Meaning: Action taken to bring a defective or nonconforming component into compliance with requirements or specifications.
Expansion: Rework is corrective effort performed after work has failed to meet defined requirements, acceptance criteria, or quality standards. In PMI usage, it represents avoidable additional work that consumes time, cost, and resources and often signals weaknesses in planning, quality management, requirements definition, or execution control. It is tracked because it affects performance, productivity, and the likelihood of meeting project objectives.
Contrast: Repair — rework restores work to meet the original requirement; repair modifies a defective item so it becomes usable, which may or may not fully restore original conformity.
Example: A software team rebuilds a reporting feature after testing shows it does not meet the approved performance and security requirements.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Rework; PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 8.2, p. 305

Core Meaning: An analytical technique used to determine the basic underlying reason that causes a variance, defect, or risk.
Expansion: Root cause analysis looks past surface symptoms to identify the underlying source of a problem or risk condition. It helps teams choose responses that address causes instead of repeatedly treating effects. PMI applies it in quality, risk, and issue contexts because durable corrective action depends on understanding why something happened in the first place.
Contrast: Variance analysis — root cause analysis seeks the underlying reason for a problem; variance analysis identifies the gap between planned and actual performance.
Example: After repeated testing failures, a project team traces the issue not to developer error but to inconsistent requirements interpretation caused by missing acceptance criteria.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition — Root cause analysis, p. 729

Core Meaning: An analytical technique used to identify the underlying cause of a problem or to examine the facets of a potential opportunity.
Expansion: This technique combines two related analytical uses in project work. Root cause analysis seeks the basic reason behind a defect, variance, or risk, while opportunity analysis explores the major aspects of a possible beneficial change or positive risk. PMI uses it to support more effective responses by addressing underlying causes and by recognising ways to improve outcomes.
Contrast: Root cause analysis — root cause analysis focuses on the source of a problem; root cause and opportunity analysis also includes structured examination of positive opportunities.
Example: After repeated rework on a design package, the team traces the issue to unclear approval criteria and also analyses whether standardising templates could create a time-saving opportunity.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, section 7.8.2.10

Core Meaning: A control chart heuristic that flags seven consecutive points on the same side of the mean as a likely non-random pattern.
Expansion: The rule of seven is a rule of thumb used in statistical process control: when seven or more consecutive data points fall on one side of the mean, the process is treated as out of control even if no point breaches the control limits. The pattern suggests an assignable (special) cause rather than normal random variation and should be investigated. It helps detect drift or bias that single-point checks would miss.
Contrast: Control limits - a point outside the control limits signals an out-of-control process directly, whereas the rule of seven detects non-random runs that stay within the limits.
Example: On a control chart tracking weld defects, seven straight measurements above the mean prompt the team to investigate a possible equipment problem.
Source: PMBOK Guide - control chart / rule of seven

Core Meaning: A subset of a population selected to draw conclusions about the whole.
Expansion: A sample is a representative portion of a population chosen for inspection or measurement so that quality can be assessed without examining every item. Sample size and selection method affect how confidently results can be generalized.
Contrast: A sample is a part, whereas the population is the entire set it is meant to represent.
Example: Pulling fifty boards from a run of ten thousand to test for defects creates the sample.
Source: Statistical method applied in quality control; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A quality tool that displays the relationship between two variables.
Expansion: A scatter diagram plots paired data points to help reveal whether two variables appear to be correlated. Project teams use it in quality analysis to explore possible cause and effect relationships before drawing stronger conclusions.
Contrast: Histogram — a scatter diagram shows the relationship between two variables; a histogram shows the distribution of one variable.
Example: A construction team plots concrete curing temperature against defect rate to see whether higher temperatures are associated with more cracking.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Scatter diagram, page 535

Core Meaning: A set of simple graphical and analytical techniques used to identify, analyse, and solve quality-related problems.
Expansion: The seven basic quality tools are commonly used to understand process behaviour, identify causes of defects, and support quality improvement. In PMI practice, they support quality planning, quality control, and root-cause analysis. They are best treated as a practical toolkit rather than a single formal PMI glossary term.
Contrast: Quality management tools — the seven basic quality tools are a specific subset of techniques; quality management tools is the broader category.
Example: A project team uses a cause-and-effect diagram and a Pareto chart to identify the main sources of defects in a testing process.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A high-level process summary that captures Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, and Customers on a single diagram.
Expansion: SIPOC is a Lean Six Sigma tool that frames a process at a glance by naming its suppliers, the inputs they provide, the major process steps, the outputs produced, and the customers who receive them. It is typically created early in an improvement effort to establish scope and shared understanding before detailed analysis. By forcing teams to identify customers and suppliers explicitly, it clarifies boundaries and accountability.
Contrast: SIPOC stays at a summary altitude of five to seven steps, while a detailed process map decomposes each step.
Example: A Six Sigma team builds a SIPOC for the invoicing process to agree on its start and end points before measuring defects.
Source: Lean Six Sigma; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A data-driven quality improvement approach that reduces variation and defects in processes to improve performance and customer outcomes.
Expansion: Six Sigma uses statistical analysis and disciplined problem solving to identify root causes of defects and improve process capability. In PMI usage, it is commonly referenced as a quality and process-improvement method that can complement project and operational work. It is most often applied where process stability, measurable performance, and defect reduction are important.
Contrast: Lean — Six Sigma primarily targets variation and defects through statistical control, while Lean primarily targets waste and flow efficiency.
Example: A manufacturing improvement project uses Six Sigma analysis to reduce assembly defects by identifying the process step that creates the highest variation.

Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A source of process variation that is not inherent to the system and signals an assignable problem.
Expansion: A special cause (or assignable cause) is variation arising from a specific, unusual factor rather than the normal operation of the process. On a control chart it shows up as points outside the control limits or as non-random patterns within them, indicating the process is out of control and needs investigation. Identifying and removing special causes restores the process to a stable, predictable state.
Contrast: Common cause - common-cause variation is the random noise inherent in a stable process, whereas special-cause variation comes from an identifiable, external disturbance.
Example: A sudden spike in defects traced to a miscalibrated machine is a special cause.
Source: PMBOK Guide - control chart / special cause variation

Core Meaning: The upper and lower bounds that define the acceptable range of performance or characteristics for a product, process, or result.
Expansion: Specification limits express what outcomes are acceptable from the customer's or requirements perspective. In PMI quality contexts, they are distinct from observed process behaviour because they define the tolerance range a deliverable must satisfy. They are used to judge conformance and support decisions about acceptance, rejection, or corrective action.
Contrast: Control limits — specification limits define acceptable performance requirements; control limits reflect the natural variation of a process as observed through statistical control.
Example: A manufacturing-related project specifies that a component diameter must remain within an approved tolerance band, and any unit outside those limits is treated as nonconforming.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), quality management concept; exact page citation not confirmed in available PMI search results

Core Meaning: A standard is an established norm, requirement, or basis for comparison that guides professional practice.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a standard provides an authoritative basis for consistent project management practice and shared understanding across practitioners and organisations. Standards may define principles, processes, frameworks, or terminology rather than prescribing a single way to run every project. In project work, they matter because they support common expectations, comparability, governance, and professional discipline.
Contrast: Methodology — a standard sets authoritative guidance or requirements, while a methodology is a specific system of practices and procedures used to perform the work.
Example: A PMO aligns its delivery governance to PMI's Standard for Project Management so project teams use a shared basis for planning, oversight, and decision-making.
Source: The Standard for Project Management in A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) – Seventh Edition, pp. 3-4

Core Meaning: A statistical measure of how widely values are dispersed around the mean.
Expansion: Standard deviation indicates the degree of variability in a set of values. In projects, it is used in estimating and quantitative analysis to describe uncertainty in durations, costs, or other forecasted results. A lower standard deviation means results cluster more tightly around the average, while a higher one indicates greater spread and less predictability.
Contrast: Variance — variance measures average squared dispersion, while standard deviation is its square root and is expressed in the same units as the original values.
Example: A project analyst calculates the standard deviation of task duration estimates to understand how much schedule uncertainty exists around the planned completion date.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, standard deviation, page not provided in available search results

Core Meaning: Establishing and applying consistent rules, methods, or specifications so that work and outputs become uniform and repeatable.
Expansion: Standardization reduces variation by defining a single agreed way to perform a task or produce a deliverable, which improves predictability and quality. In project and operational settings it underpins economies of scale, easier training, and reliable measurement.
Contrast: Opposite of customization, which tailors each output to a specific case at the cost of repeatability.
Example: A PMO standardizes status report templates across all projects so executives can compare performance consistently.
Source: ISO 9000 quality management

Core Meaning: A method of selecting part of a population for inspection or analysis in order to draw conclusions about the whole.
Expansion: Statistical sampling is used when checking every item would be inefficient, costly, or unnecessary. In project work it is most often applied in quality planning and quality control to inspect a representative subset. The approach supports evidence-based decisions while reducing the effort of full inspection.
Contrast: Inspection — statistical sampling examines a subset of items, while inspection may refer to examining all items or the inspection activity more broadly.
Example: A construction project samples concrete test cylinders from each pour rather than testing every unit produced.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition — statistical sampling, p. 304

Core Meaning: The degree to which a system operates effectively against its expected functional and nonfunctional measures under real conditions.
Expansion: In project settings, system performance refers to how well a completed or evolving system meets expected levels such as speed, reliability, capacity, accuracy, or stability. It is assessed against defined criteria, operational needs, and acceptance thresholds to determine whether the delivered solution is fit for use. The concept matters particularly in technical and engineering projects where benefits depend not just on delivery of the system, but on how well it actually performs.
Contrast: Project performance — system performance concerns how the delivered solution operates; project performance concerns how the project itself performs against scope, schedule, cost, and similar measures.
Example: After deploying a customer portal, the team verifies that average response times remain within target levels even during peak user traffic.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A test performed on the complete integrated system to evaluate whether it meets specified requirements.
Expansion: System test examines the fully combined system rather than isolated components to confirm that end-to-end behaviour aligns with functional and nonfunctional expectations. In project delivery, it is typically conducted after integration and before final acceptance or release so defects can be identified at the whole-system level. It is especially important where multiple subsystems, interfaces, and operational scenarios must work together reliably.
Contrast: Integration test — integration testing checks interactions among combined components; system testing evaluates the complete integrated system against overall requirements.
Example: Before go-live on an ERP implementation, the team runs system tests covering order entry, inventory updates, invoicing, security roles, and batch processing across the full platform.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The degree to which project work and deliverables meet the required technical specifications and performance criteria.
Expansion: Technical performance refers to how well the product, service, or result performs against defined technical requirements. It is monitored to confirm that the project is producing outputs that satisfy design intent, quality expectations, and acceptance criteria. PMI includes technical performance analysis as a way to compare actual technical results with planned or required performance.
Contrast: Technical debt — technical performance measures current conformance to requirements, while technical debt reflects future rework or compromise created by expedient design choices.
Example: A software release is tested to confirm response time, throughput, and error rates meet the agreed performance targets before acceptance.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition / Process Groups A Practice Guide — Technical performance analysis, p. 205

Core Meaning: Technical performance measures are indicators that track whether a product, system, or project deliverable is achieving required technical parameters over time.
Expansion: These measures compare actual technical achievement against planned specifications or thresholds, such as weight, speed, capacity, reliability, or response time. They help teams detect whether solution performance is drifting away from requirements before the issue becomes a late-stage quality or acceptance problem. In practice they are especially useful in engineering, construction, defense, technology, and product-development projects where technical compliance must be monitored progressively.
Contrast: Key performance indicator — a technical performance measure tracks achievement of technical characteristics of the deliverable; a KPI may track broader business or project performance.
Example: On an aerospace project, the team tracks engine temperature tolerance, fuel efficiency, and structural weight against design thresholds at each prototype review.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition — Monitor Communications and Monitor Risks glossary usage; also formal term in PMI Lexicon, exact page varies by edition

Core Meaning: Project documents that define how deliverables will be checked against quality requirements.
Expansion: Test and evaluation documents describe the activities, criteria, and records used to verify that deliverables meet quality goals, and they capture results during control quality. They are created when managing quality and provide evidence of conformance.
Contrast: Quality Checklists - a quality checklist is a simple list of items to verify, whereas test and evaluation documents are the broader set defining and recording how testing is done.
Example: Test plans, inspection sheets, and evaluation reports prepared for a new medical device.
Source: PMBOK Guide - test and evaluation documents

Core Meaning: A test plan is a document that describes the scope, approach, resources, schedule, and criteria for testing a product or deliverable.
Expansion: It defines what will be tested, how testing will be performed, who is responsible, and what conditions must be met for acceptance or completion. In project management it supports quality planning and control by making verification activities explicit rather than ad hoc. Test plans are especially visible in software, systems, product-development, and regulated environments, but the concept applies wherever deliverables require structured validation.
Contrast: Quality management plan — a test plan focuses specifically on testing activities for deliverables; a quality management plan governs the broader approach to quality policies, standards, assurance, and control.
Example: For a payroll-system implementation, the team creates a test plan covering unit, integration, user acceptance, data-migration, and regression testing, with entry and exit criteria for each phase.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The evaluation of a product, deliverable, or component to determine whether it meets specified requirements and works as intended.
Expansion: Testing is a quality-related activity used to verify whether deliverables conform to defined requirements, acceptance criteria, or performance expectations. In project management it commonly appears in product development, implementation, transition, and validation work. PMI treats it as part of assuring and controlling quality rather than as a substitute for clear requirements or stakeholder acceptance.
Contrast: Inspection — testing is a specific evaluative technique to check behaviour or performance, while inspection is the broader examination of a work product for compliance.
Example: Before a new payroll system goes live, the project team executes user acceptance testing to confirm pay rules, approvals, and reports function correctly.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An organization-wide philosophy of continuous improvement that engages everyone in raising quality.
Expansion: TQM is a management approach in which all members of an organization continuously improve processes, products, and culture to meet or exceed customer expectations. It stresses customer focus, employee involvement, fact-based decisions, and ongoing process improvement. It influenced modern quality practice and underlies tools such as the Deming cycle.
Contrast: Six Sigma - Six Sigma is a data-driven methodology focused on reducing defects and variation, whereas TQM is a broader organization-wide quality philosophy.
Example: A manufacturer empowers every employee to suggest process improvements and tracks customer satisfaction as a company-wide goal.
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality management theories (TQM)

Core Meaning: A test that verifies a small, isolated part of a product or solution works as intended.
Expansion: A unit test checks the behavior of a discrete component, function, or module independently from the rest of the system. It helps teams find defects early, support frequent integration, and maintain confidence in ongoing changes. In practice it is most associated with software and digital product work, especially in iterative delivery environments.
Contrast: Integration test — a unit test checks one isolated component, while an integration test checks how multiple components work together.
Example: A development team creates automated tests for a tax calculation function so each code change can be verified before being merged.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, glossary — unit test

Core Meaning: The degree to which a product or solution can be used effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily by intended users.
Expansion: Usability concerns how easy and effective a product is for real users to learn, operate, and complete tasks with. In projects it matters because technically correct outputs can still fail if users struggle to adopt them or make errors when using them. Usability is especially relevant in product development, digital transformation, service design, and operational handoff.
Contrast: Functionality — functionality is what the solution can do, while usability is how easily and successfully people can use it.
Example: A public-sector portal project revises its form workflow after usability testing shows citizens cannot complete the application without repeated help.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A test performed by users or customer representatives to determine whether a solution meets their needs and is acceptable for use.
Expansion: User acceptance testing checks whether the delivered product or service works for the people who will use it in practice. It is typically performed near the end of development or before release, after technical testing has already been completed. The focus is on business fit and usability from the user's perspective rather than on internal technical correctness.
Contrast: System test — user acceptance test checks whether users accept the solution for business use; system test checks whether the system meets technical and functional specifications.
Example: Before go-live, business users run scripted scenarios in the new payroll system to confirm that pay calculations, approvals, and reports meet their needs.
Source: PMI Lexicon — user acceptance test

Core Meaning: The evaluation of whether a product, service, or result meets the needs of the customer and other identified stakeholders.
Expansion: Validation focuses on fitness for use from the stakeholder perspective. It asks whether the delivered outcome solves the right problem and satisfies intended needs, not just whether it conforms to specifications. In PMI usage it is contrasted with verification, which checks compliance with requirements or specifications. Validation is commonly associated with reviews, demonstrations, testing with users, and acceptance activities.
Contrast: Verification — validation checks whether the result meets stakeholder needs; verification checks whether it complies with requirements or specifications.
Example: A product team demonstrates a new claims workflow to end users and confirms it supports their actual process before release.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Validation

Core Meaning: Quality sampling that measures a characteristic on a continuous scale rather than as pass or fail.
Expansion: Variables sampling assesses quality by measuring a quantifiable attribute, such as length or weight, and recording the actual value on a continuous scale. It yields more information per sample than attribute sampling and supports analysis of how far results deviate from target. It is used when the degree of conformance, not just conformance itself, matters.
Contrast: Unlike attributes sampling, which records a simple conforms or does-not-conform result, variables sampling captures the measured degree of conformance.
Example: Measuring the exact diameter of sampled bolts in millimeters is variables sampling, versus merely judging each bolt good or bad.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The evaluation of whether a product, service, or result complies with a regulation, requirement, specification, or imposed condition.
Expansion: Verification checks conformance against defined criteria. It is concerned with whether the work product was built correctly according to the agreed requirements, specifications, or standards. PMI contrasts it with validation, which asks whether the result is the right one for stakeholder needs. Verification is often performed through inspections, tests, walkthroughs, and reviews.
Contrast: Validation — verification checks conformance to requirements or specifications; validation checks whether the result meets stakeholder needs.
Example: A quality team tests a software release against the approved requirements specification to confirm each function behaves as designed.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Verification

Core Meaning: A structured review in which a work product is presented step by step for feedback.
Expansion: In a walkthrough the author guides reviewers through a document, design, or code to find issues and build shared understanding. It is typically informal compared with a formal inspection and relies on the author leading the session.
Contrast: Inspection — a walkthrough is author-led and informal; an inspection is a formal, role-based defect-detection review.
Example: The analyst runs a requirements walkthrough with stakeholders to confirm each item before sign-off.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: A control chart used to monitor the average of a process over time.
Expansion: An X-bar control chart plots sample means against control limits to show whether a process average is stable or shifting. It is used in quality management to distinguish normal variation from signals that may require investigation. In project work, it supports monitoring and controlling process performance where repeated measurements are available.
Contrast: Control chart — an X-bar control chart is a specific control chart for monitoring sample means, while control chart is the broader category.
Example: In a construction materials project, the quality team tracks the average compressive strength of sampled concrete batches each day to detect process drift.

Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition — see glossary entry for control chart and discussion of quality data representation

Core Meaning: An informal review approach in which work is checked in alternating passes or perspectives to improve completeness and consistency.
Expansion: Teams may use a zigzag review pattern to move back and forth across sections, disciplines, or criteria instead of reviewing strictly from start to finish. It is a practitioner expression rather than a formal PMI glossary entry.
Contrast: Sequential review — zigzag review alternates across areas or viewpoints; sequential review follows one linear pass from start to finish.
Example: A team reviews a requirements package by alternating between business rules and system rules to catch mismatches early.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Exploratory testing is a flexible testing approach where testers learn, design tests, and execute tests at the same time.
Expansion: Exploratory testing is useful when requirements are evolving, time is limited, or scripted tests may miss important behavior. It relies on skilled judgment, product learning, and rapid feedback rather than a fully predefined test script. In agile and adaptive work, it complements automated and acceptance testing by helping the team discover defects, usability issues, and edge cases earlier.
Contrast: Automated testing - automated testing repeats predefined checks, while exploratory testing investigates the product interactively.
Example: During a mobile app sprint, a tester explores the checkout flow on different network speeds and finds that the payment screen can freeze after a timeout.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Risk-based testing prioritizes test effort toward the product areas most likely to threaten objectives if they fail.
Expansion: Risk-based testing applies probability and impact thinking to quality work. The team gives more attention to functions, integrations, or scenarios with higher failure exposure, while lower-risk areas may receive lighter checks. It helps project teams use limited test time where defects would have the greatest business, safety, schedule, or customer impact.
Contrast: Risk prioritization - risk prioritization ranks project risks overall, while risk-based testing applies that logic specifically to test coverage.
Example: A banking project tests authentication, transfer limits, and audit logs before low-risk profile display changes because failures there could affect compliance and customer funds.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Coding standards are agreed rules for writing code so a development team can keep the product understandable, maintainable, and consistent.
Expansion: In agile software work, coding standards reduce friction because any team member can read, modify, test, and refactor another person's code more easily. They support quality management by making good practice explicit instead of relying on individual habits.
Contrast: Code review - coding standards define the expected rules; code review checks whether work follows them and meets quality expectations.
Example: A mobile app team agrees on naming, formatting, test structure, and error-handling conventions before several developers build related payment features.
Source: PMI Agile Practice Guide - Extreme Programming practices

Core Meaning: A run chart plots process data in time order to reveal trends, shifts, or other nonrandom patterns.
Expansion: A run chart is a simple quality and process-improvement display that shows how a measure changes over time. It helps a project team see whether recent performance is improving, worsening, or varying in a pattern worth investigating before adding formal statistical control limits.
Contrast: Control chart - a run chart shows time-ordered variation, while a control chart adds statistically calculated control limits to judge process stability.
Example: A team plots daily escaped defects for a software release and notices a sustained upward run after a new integration is added.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition - data gathering and analysis in the Measurement Performance Domain

Core Meaning: A root-cause questioning technique that repeatedly asks why a problem occurred until the underlying cause, not just the symptom, is exposed.
Expansion: Starting from a defined problem, the team asks why it happened, then why that answer is true, continuing through roughly five iterations or until a controllable root cause appears. It is simple, conversational, and pairs well with other quality tools such as fishbone diagrams. Five is a guide, not a rigid count—stop when further whys stop adding useful insight. In projects it supports defect analysis, process improvement, and retrospective learning.
Contrast: Fishbone diagram — a fishbone structures many possible cause categories visually; Five Whys drills one causal chain depth-first with sequential why questions.
Example: A sprint demo fails. Why? Bad build. Why? Integration test skipped. Why? Environment down. Why? Shared test server not restored. Why? No owner for environment recovery—assign ownership and a restore checklist.
Source: Quality management practice used with root cause analysis (commonly referenced alongside PMI quality tools; not a standalone PMI Lexicon head term)

Core Meaning: A lean practice in which leaders go to the place where work actually happens to observe, listen, and learn before deciding how to improve.
Expansion: Gemba means the real place of value creation—the shop floor, project war room, customer site, or team workspace. Leaders walk the gemba to see process reality, talk with people doing the work, and surface problems that metrics alone may hide. The intent is respectful inquiry and continuous improvement, not inspection for blame. Findings feed kaizen, root-cause work, and better flow decisions.
Contrast: Inspection — inspection verifies product or deliverable conformance; a gemba walk observes the work system and people to understand causes and improve the process.
Example: A project manager spends an hour at the integration test bay, watches handoffs between developers and testers, and hears that nightly builds fail because of unclear environment ownership—then sponsors a fix to the build pipeline.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A process flowchart that organizes steps into lanes by role, team, or department so ownership and handoffs are visible.
Expansion: Each lane represents a participant (person, role, or function), and activities sit in the lane of the party that performs them. Arrows show sequence and transfers between lanes, which makes bottlenecks, redundant loops, and unclear ownership easier to spot than on a plain flowchart. Teams use swimlane diagrams in process analysis, requirements workshops, and improvement work to clarify who does what before designing or changing a process.
Contrast: Flowchart - a flowchart shows the sequence of steps; a swimlane diagram adds who is responsible for each step by separating work into role or department lanes.
Example: Before automating order fulfillment, the team maps request, pick, pack, and ship steps in lanes for Customer Service, Warehouse, and Logistics to expose double entry and long handoff waits.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry
